retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 deficiency has 4 primary etiologies:

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the pathophysiology of vitamin B12 deficiency. Review the risk factors for developing a vitamin B12 deficiency. Describe the typical presentation of a patient with vitamin B12 deficiency. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients with vitamin B12 deficiency. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dietary insufficiency: Vitamin B12 is stored in excess in the liver; however, patients who have followed a strict vegan diet for approximately 3 years may develop a B12 deficiency from a lack of dietary intake.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Serum B12 and folate levels also should be obtained. Folic acid deficiency also presents as macrocytic anemia and is often confused with B12 deficiency. Ordering serum levels of both B12 and folate can help differentiate between the two disease processes. A serum B12 above 300 pg/mL is interpreted as normal. Patients with B12 levels between 200 and 300 pg/mL are considered borderline, and further enzymatic testing may be helpful in diagnosis. Patients with B12 levels below 200 pg/mL are considered deficient. However, a low serum B12 level does not determine the etiology of the deficiency. If the etiology is uncertain, further testing should be done to investigate. Further enzymatic testing should be performed in patients with borderline B12 levels (200 to 300 pg/mL). As described, B12 deficiency results in the accumulation of MMA and homocysteine. Thus, serum levels of MMA and homocysteine should both be elevated in cases of B12 deficiency. These lab values can also help to distinguish B12 deficiency from folate deficiency, in which homocysteine levels are elevated but MMA levels are normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A physician is responsible for the diagnosis, evaluation, proper treatment, and tailor therapy of patients for an individual basis. Given the array of causes for vitamin B-12 (cobalamin) deficiency, interprofessional collaboration is often required for diagnosis, treatment, and improving patient outcomes with this condition. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biotin Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue and generalized weakness Hair loss or thinning Skin changes such as rash, especially around orifices Neurologic symptoms such as paresthesias, muscle pain, or seizures in severe cases The physical examination findings reflect multisystem involvement and may include: Dermatologic: Diffuse or patchy alopecia Seborrheic, eczematous, or scaly rash, often involving periorificial areas (around eyes, nose, mouth) and intertriginous zones Conjunctivitis or red eyes Neurologic: Hypotonia and decreased muscle tone Peripheral neuropathy signs, such as reduced sensation or paresthesia Developmental delay or cognitive impairment in infants and young children Seizures in severe or untreated cases Other findings: Signs of metabolic acidosis in severe cases (tachypnea, lethargy) Generalized weakness and poor feeding in infants

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Neurological symptoms are typically absent in folate deficiency (unlike vitamin B12 deficiency). Anemia without neurological symptoms suggests folate deficiency. Anemia with neurological symptoms suggests a vitamin B12 deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Toxin exposure: Exposure to nitrous oxide can cause vitamin B12 deficiency and neurologic symptoms. Metformin treatment can cause B12 deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Biotin Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Neurological symptoms include hypotonia, seizures, ataxia, numbness and tingling of the extremities, intellectual disability, and developmental delay in children. The patients may exhibit depression, lethargy, or report a history of hallucinations. Additional neurological abnormalities include optic atrophy and sensorineural hearing loss in untreated patients. [9] Intestinal symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and anorexia may also develop in patients with biotin deficiency. Other presenting signs include ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, and organic aciduria. [9] A detailed history should focus on identifying risk factors:

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: For patients who are promptly treated with vitamin B12, the prognosis is good. In general, younger patients have better outcomes compared to older individuals. The best response is obtained in people with the absence of severe neurological deficits.

retrieved_docs.title: Megaloblastic Anemia -- Evaluation -- The 2014 Guidelines from the British Committee for Standards in Haematology [23]
retrieved_docs.content: Neurological symptoms due to vitamin B12 deficiency can occur in the absence of macrocytosis. In unexplained neurological symptoms consistent with B12 deficiency, cobalamin assays should be obtained. (Grade 1B)

retrieved_docs.title: Pernicious Anemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with serum cobalamin levels ≥200 ng/L diagnostic performance may be enhanced by measuring serum MMA, fasting homocysteine, and HTC. [1] Elevated MMA and/or fasting homocysteine are indicators of B12 deficiency in patients without evidence of impaired renal function or an inherited cobalamin processing enzyme defect. MMA is more sensitive and more specific, and elevation is seen only in patients with B12 deficiency. Homocysteine levels may also increase in folate deficiency, pyridoxine deficiency, and hypothyroidism. Serum HTC levels may be used for assessing B12 status, with or without MMA and/or homocysteine, and may reflect B12 absorptive capacity. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Megaloblastic Anemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: It is imperative to remember that vitamin B12 and folate deficiency testing should be done simultaneously to ensure both deficiencies are diagnosed if present. In cases where folate is replaced without vitamin B12 supplementation and underlying B12 deficiency, the neurologic manifestations of vitamin B12 deficiency will not be treated and may potentially get worse. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Pernicious Anemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pernicious anemia is a relatively rare autoimmune disorder that causes diminishment in dietary vitamin B12 (cobalamin) absorption, resulting in B12 deficiency and subsequent megaloblastic anemia. It affects people of all ages worldwide, particularly those over 60. Despite the advances in understanding, making the diagnosis can be challenging for clinicians due to its complexity, broad spectrum of clinical presentation, and limitations of the currently available diagnostic tests. Once diagnosed, prompt treatment with B12 supplementation commonly reverses the patient's anemia; however, they will require lifelong supplementation and monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 954
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as belonging to the broader category of Other deficiency anemias. The category Other deficiency anemias encompasses several specific deficiency disorders, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الوبيل (a form of vitamin‑B12‑related anemia), and فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia). In addition, Other deficiency anemias has a child condition called فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (non‑megaloblastic anemia). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease falls within this group of deficiency‑related anemias guides clinicians to assess for underlying nutrient deficiencies and related subtypes when diagnosing and treating affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2277
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is categorized under the broader group of Other deficiency anemias. The category Other deficiency anemias encompasses several specific types, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الوبيل (a form of vitamin B12‑related anemia), and فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia). In addition, Other deficiency anemias has a child entity فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, which refers to acquired hemolytic anemia. This hierarchical classification highlights that anemia associated with chronic kidney disease is considered part of a spectrum of deficiency‑related anemias, alongside distinct nutritional, iron‑related, and acquired hemolytic forms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2242
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is a specific form of vitamin B12 that is commonly grouped with folic acid in clinical contexts, reflecting the practice of assessing both nutrients together. Mecobalamin, also known as methylcobalamin, is a distinct methylated derivative of vitamin B12 and is considered a member of the broader vitamin B12 family. Laboratory testing that measures vitamin B12 and folic acid often uses a combined assay described as “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型,” which quantifies cyanocobalamin (a vitamin B12 analogue) and folic acid (叶酸) in serum or whole‑blood specimens and reports the results as nominal values. This integrated measurement approach aids clinicians in evaluating a patient’s B12 and folate status simultaneously, supporting more comprehensive nutritional assessment and guiding appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12, encompassing cyanocobalamin and its analogues, includes several distinct cobalamin forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, which are recognized as member compounds within this vitamin family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: combined vitamin‑B formulations (e.g., folic acid 1 mg + vitamin B12 0.5 mg), and distinct vitamin B12 analogues (cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin)—are organized within broader drug classes, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate supplementation and therapy regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2474
retrieved_docs.content: Other deficiency anemias is a broad category of anemia that is classified as فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia) and فقر الدم الوبيل (pernicious anemia). Within this category, it has a child subgroup called فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (non‑megaloblastic anemia), and it also has a relationship with فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑type hemolytic anemia). These connections illustrate that other deficiency anemias encompass several specific forms of anemia, ranging from nutrient‑deficiency–related types to a hemolytic variant, highlighting the diversity of mechanisms and classifications relevant to diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
retrieved_docs.content: B₁₂ family, mecobalamin and hydroxocobalamin are members of “Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues),” reflecting alternative cobalamin analogues used in supplementation and therapy. Overall, the taxonomy connects specific vitamin forms, iron compounds, and combined supplement products to the categories of antianemic therapy, iron supplementation, and the prevention or treatment of nutritional deficiencies. This structured framework enables clinicians to select formulations that simultaneously address iron deficiency, vitamin‑dependent metabolic needs, and broader micronutrient gaps, particularly in populations with increased iron requirements, malabsorption risk, or postoperative bariatric status.

The oral tablet “folic acid 1 mg / vitamin B₁₂ 0.5 mg” exemplifies this subclass; it lists vitamin B₁₂ and folic acid as ingredients (has_ingredient) and is measured in serum or whole‑blood samples (measures). The Multivitamin Drug Class provides an organizational layer that connects generic multivitamin preparations to the folic‑acid/vitamin B₁₂ tablet via an “inverse_isa” relationship, indicating that the tablet belongs to this drug class, and also maintains a direct “has relationship” link to the generic multivitamin preparation, thereby unifying individual products within a cohesive therapeutic category. In the vitamin B₁₂ family, cyanocobalamin and its analogues constitute a group of cobalamin compounds. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1397
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B1, plain is classified as a member of the thiamin family and is identified as a form of vitamin B1 that can be administered alone or in combination with vitamin B6 and vitamin B12. This designation highlights that plain vitamin B1 is both a specific thiamine compound and a component of multi‑vitamin regimens that include other B‑complex vitamins, underscoring its role in nutritional supplementation and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified under the “Other deficiency anemias” group, which also comprises folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), vitamin‑B12‑related anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات), thalassemia (الثلاسيميا), non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية), and hereditary hemolytic syndromes such as hereditary spherocytosis‑like disorder (متلازمة انحلال الدم الوريمي). Thalassemia is linked clinically to sickle‑cell‑related retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية), indicating a risk of retinal vascular complications similar to those seen in sickle cell disease. retrieved_docs.content: D), and ascorbic acid (vitamin C) with its combination products (e.g., ascorbic acid + calcium). B‑vitamin groups are represented by the vitamin B complex, riboflavin, vitamin B₁ (alone or combined with B₆ and B₁₂), vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin, hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin) and folic acid; “folic acid / vitamin B₁₂” and “folic acid + cyanocobalamin” are children of “vitaminy”. Hydroxokobalamin is classified as a form of vitamin B₁₂, reinforcing its placement within the B₁₂ family. Choline is also a child of “vitaminy”, reflecting its inclusion in the vitamin‑related nutrient group. Minerals are related to “vitaminy” and to an undefined chemical category (“كيمياء غير محدد”), which is also linked to nutritional deficiency (“نقص التغذية”). Iron‑containing multivitamin preparations are noted as ingredients of vitamin K‑containing products. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as agents containing vitamin B12 and folic acid, and they are specifically described as folic‑acid/vitamin B12 formulations. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is likewise placed within the broader category of vitamin B12 and folic‑acid compounds. Within this vitamin B12 group, individual forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin are recognized as distinct members. Clinical assessment often includes laboratory measures that quantify both folic acid and cyanocobalamin (the active form of vitamin B12) in serum or whole‑blood specimens, providing a nominal‑type impression of the patient’s nutritional status. Together, these relationships clarify that antianemic therapy commonly relies on combined vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplementation, that various biochemical forms of vitamin B12 (including mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin) are part of this therapeutic class, and that combined measurement of folic acid and cyanocobalamin is a key tool for monitoring treatment effectiveness and guiding patient care.
